newsDiscoveryHints.geographicHint: NC-11 spans 16 western North Carolina counties/partial-counties anchored by Buncombe County (Asheville), the district's population center, extending west to the Smoky Mountains (Cherokee, Graham, Swain) and southeast toward Henderson and Transylvania (Hendersonville, Brevard); boundaries unchanged by SB 249 / SL 2025-95.

ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: North Carolina's 2026 statewide primary. Chuck Edwards (R, inc.) won the GOP primary with roughly 70% over Adam Smith; Jamie Ager (D) won a 5-way Democratic primary with roughly 65% over Richard Hudspeth, Chris Harjes, Zelda Briarwood, and Morris 'Moe' Davis. Polls open 6:30 a.m.-7:30 p.m.
ELECTION_EVENTS.governingAuthority: North Carolina State Board of Elections

ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: A second (runoff) primary is triggered only if the leading primary candidate receives less than 30% of the vote (lowered from 40% by SB 656 in 2017), and only if requested by the second-place finisher within 9 days of the primary. Not triggered in NC-11: both Edwards (~70%) and Ager (65%) cleared the threshold comfortably in their respective primaries.
ELECTION_EVENTS.governingAuthority: North Carolina State Board of Elections

ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: Last day to register online or by mail for the November 3, 2026 general election, 5:00 p.m. (25 days before Election Day). Same-day registration remains available in person during the early-voting window.
ELECTION_EVENTS.governingAuthority: North Carolina State Board of Elections

ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: Early in-person voting begins statewide, including all NC-11 counties. Same-day voter registration is available in person during this window for voters who missed the standard registration deadline (they must provide proof of residence).
ELECTION_EVENTS.governingAuthority: North Carolina State Board of Elections

ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: Deadline to submit a mail absentee ballot request for the November 3, 2026 general election, 5:00 p.m.
ELECTION_EVENTS.governingAuthority: North Carolina State Board of Elections

ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: Last day of early in-person voting, closing at 3:00 p.m. on the final Saturday before Election Day.
ELECTION_EVENTS.governingAuthority: North Carolina State Board of Elections

ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: U.S. midterm general election. NC-11: Chuck Edwards (R, inc.) vs. Jamie Ager (D). Polls open 6:30 a.m.-7:30 p.m. NC-11's boundaries were unchanged by SB 249 / SL 2025-95 (Oct. 2025), unlike neighboring NC-01 and NC-03. A July 2, 2026 BPR analysis characterized the race as a potential toss-up despite Edwards' incumbency; this is political framing, not a procedural fact.
ELECTION_EVENTS.governingAuthority: North Carolina State Board of Elections

ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: Absentee ballots must be received (not merely postmarked) by the county board of elections by 7:30 p.m. on Election Day. North Carolina eliminated its post-Election-Day mail-receipt grace period for domestic ballots under SB 747 (2023); UOCAVA (military/overseas) ballots retain a longer receipt window.
ELECTION_EVENTS.governingAuthority: North Carolina State Board of Elections

ELECTION_EVENTS.significance: Voters who cast a provisional ballot due to lacking accepted photo ID must cure it — by providing ID or completing an ID Exception Form — by noon on the day before the county canvass (noon, Nov. 12, 2026, ahead of the Nov. 13 canvass).
County boards of elections meet at 11:00 a.m. to canvass and certify county-level results, including provisional and cured ballots.
The North Carolina State Board of Elections meets at 11:00 a.m. to certify final statewide results, including the NC-11 general election outcome.
ELECTION_EVENTS.governingAuthority: North Carolina State Board of Elections

RULES.currentRule: Photo ID is required to vote in person and by mail, per SB 747 (2023), in effect since 2023 and confirmed for 2026. Accepted forms: NC driver's license or non-driver ID, another state's driver's license (if registered to vote within 90 days of the election), U.S. passport, military/veteran ID, tribal enrollment card, an NCSBE-approved student or employee ID, or a free NC voter photo ID from the county board of elections. A voter without acceptable ID casts a provisional ballot and must cure it by providing ID or completing an ID Exception Form by noon on the day before the county canvass.
RULES.whyItMatters: NC-11 voters across all 16 counties/partial-counties must present qualifying photo ID at the polls or with their absentee ballot. Voters lacking ID have a narrow but workable cure window (through noon on Nov. 12, 2026 for the general election) to avoid having their provisional ballot rejected.
RULES.detail: SB 747 (2023). Cure deadline: noon on the day before county canvass (noon Nov. 12, 2026, ahead of the Nov. 13 canvass). Identical statewide rule as NC-01/NC-03.

RULES.currentRule: North Carolina does not allow absentee ballot drop boxes. SB 747 (2023) explicitly bans them statewide; ballots must be returned by mail or in person to the county board of elections office.
RULES.whyItMatters: NC-11 voters cannot rely on a drop box network for absentee ballot return, unlike some other states. Ballots must be mailed (allowing enough time to be received by the deadline) or delivered in person to the county board of elections office.
RULES.detail: SB 747 (2023). Confirmed by multiple county board-of-elections sources per research; no dedicated NC-11 drop-box resource tile exists as a result.

RULES.topic: Absentee ballot receipt deadline — no postmark grace period (SB 747)
RULES.currentRule: Absentee ballots must be received (not merely postmarked) by the county board of elections by 7:30 p.m. on Election Day (Nov. 3, 2026). SB 747 (2023) eliminated North Carolina's post-Election-Day mail-receipt grace period for domestic ballots; UOCAVA (military/overseas) ballots retain a longer receipt window.
RULES.whyItMatters: NC-11 voters mailing an absentee ballot must ensure it physically arrives by 7:30 p.m. on Election Day — a postmark on or before Election Day is not sufficient for domestic ballots. Voters mailing close to Election Day should consider in-person return instead.
RULES.detail: SB 747 (2023). Applies statewide, including all NC-11 counties.

RULES.currentRule: Since SB 656 (2017), a second (runoff) primary is triggered only if the leading primary candidate receives less than 30% of the vote (previously 40%), and only if requested by the second-place finisher within 9 days of the primary. Not triggered in NC-11's 2026 primaries — Edwards won with roughly 70% and Ager with roughly 65%, both comfortably above the threshold.
RULES.whyItMatters: NC-11's Republican and Democratic primaries each produced a clear nominee without a runoff. This rule is relevant background for understanding how NC-11's general-election matchup (Edwards vs. Ager) was finalized.
RULES.detail: SB 656 (2017). NEEDS REVIEW: exact hour-of-day for the second-primary request deadline (9 days after the March 3 primary) was described generally per MultiState and not independently re-confirmed against NCSBE's own second-primary procedures page.

RULES.topic: NC-11 congressional map — unchanged by SB 249 / SL 2025-95
RULES.currentRule: North Carolina's congressional map was redrawn via SB 249 / Session Law 2025-95, enacted October 2025, but this act altered only NC-01 and NC-03 boundaries. NC-11's boundaries were left UNCHANGED and retain the same western North Carolina footprint used in the 2022 and 2024 elections. There is no known pending litigation affecting NC-11's map for 2026.
RULES.whyItMatters: Unlike NC-01 and NC-03, NC-11 voters are not affected by new district lines for the 2026 cycle — this reduces boundary-confusion risk for the dashboard's western NC users, though partial-county precinct lines (Clay, Macon, Mitchell, Polk, Transylvania, Yancey) still warrant confirmation via the NCSBE polling lookup tool.
RULES.detail: NEEDS REVIEW: the enacted SL 2025-95 plan PDF was not independently re-fetched from ncleg.gov in this pass; the unchanged-for-NC-11 claim is corroborated via news reporting naming only NC-01/NC-03 as altered districts. Clay, Macon, Mitchell, Polk, Transylvania, and Yancey partial-county split-boundary details were confirmed as partial via Wikipedia only, not an NCSBE/county GIS source.

RULES.topic: Same-day registration during early voting only
RULES.currentRule: North Carolina allows same-day voter registration only during the early-voting (One-Stop) period, from Oct. 15 through Oct. 31, 2026, for the general election. Voters must provide proof of residence. Standard online/mail registration closes 25 days before Election Day (Oct. 9, 2026, 5:00 p.m.); there is no same-day registration on Election Day itself at Election Day precincts.
RULES.whyItMatters: NC-11 voters who miss the Oct. 9 registration deadline have a narrower path than in some other states: they can still register and vote during the Oct. 15-31 early-voting window with proof of residence, but not on Election Day itself.
RULES.detail: NEEDS REVIEW: this rule reflects the same statewide procedure verified for NC-01/NC-03; the underlying NCSBE same-day registration procedure page was not independently re-fetched in this pass to confirm the exact proof-of-residence document list.

NEWS.headline: Democrats choose Ager in NC-11 primary; Edwards wins GOP nod
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Reports Chuck Edwards' GOP primary win (~70%) and Jamie Ager's 5-way Democratic primary win (65%), setting up the NC-11 general-election matchup.

NEWS.headline: Edwards, Ager wins set up closely watched battle for 11th District seat
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Local coverage of the NC-11 primary outcomes and the framing of the general election as closely watched.

NEWS.headline: Edwards vs. Ager: 11th District contest could be toss-up by November, experts say
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Political analysts characterize NC-11 as a potential toss-up despite Edwards' incumbency. NEEDS REVIEW: this is a single-outlet analyst characterization — contextual/political framing, not an authoritative forecast or procedural fact.

NEWS.headline: NC House passes sweeping elections bill with auditor powers, ballot challenges
NEWS.relevanceSummary: North Carolina’s House passed House Bill 958, which would expand ballot challenges, lengthen some cure deadlines, and increase state oversight of county election boards. Those changes would apply to NC-11 voters because they affect statewide ballot handling, voter-registration corrections, and election audits in all counties.

NEWS.headline: ‘Chasing ghosts': NC House approves elections bill expanding state oversight
NEWS.relevanceSummary: This report says House Bill 958 would give state officials more power over county election boards, require more extensive ballot review, and extend deadlines for curing absentee and provisional ballot issues. That matters to NC-11 voters because it changes how ballots are checked and corrected across North Carolina.

NEWS.headline: NC's 11th District could shape the 2026 Democratic wave
NEWS.relevanceSummary: The article is specifically about NC-11 and notes the district’s general-election matchup, making it relevant for voters tracking who will appear on the ballot. It is less procedural than the other items, but it still helps voters understand the district race they may vote in.

NEWS.headline: Sweeping election law bill passes key NC House committee, but more changes possible
NEWS.relevanceSummary: WRAL reports that a sweeping election-law bill advanced through a key House committee and would affect ballot counting, provisional-ballot procedures, campaign finance reporting, and county election-board conduct. Because NC-11 voters are also North Carolina voters, any statewide procedural change would directly affect how elections are run in their district.

NEWS.headline: North Carolina election board again makes it harder to vote by mail
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Democracy Docket reports that the GOP-controlled State Board of Elections approved new permanent rules requiring voters to submit written attestations to fix several mail-ballot envelope irregularities that were previously cured automatically, increasing the risk that NC‑11 voters’ mail ballots could be rejected if they do not respond.[1]

NEWS.headline: 2026 election; changes in the NC State Auditor’s office; new ballot rules | State Lines
NEWS.sourceName: YouTube – State Lines / PBS North Carolina
NEWS.relevanceSummary: In this State Lines public-affairs segment, panelists discuss the new State Board rule allowing county election boards to reject reasonable-impediment affidavits for lack of photo ID by simple majority, clarifying how the changed standard could affect voters who appear without ID in upcoming elections across North Carolina, including NC‑11.[5]

NEWS.headline: Elecciones en Carolina del Norte: Junta Estatal aprueba cambios en boletas provisionales y restricciones al sonido
NEWS.relevanceSummary: Enlace Latino NC explains that the State Board removed the requirement for county boards to be unanimous in rejecting provisional ballots lacking photo ID and adopted restrictions on amplified sound near polling places, both of which will shape how provisional votes and polling-place environments are handled for NC‑11 voters.[2]

NEWS.headline: North Carolina Elections Board Votes to Require Only a Majority, Not Unanimity, to Reject Voter Photo-ID Exception Affidavits
NEWS.relevanceSummary: This article details the July 16 decision by the North Carolina State Board of Elections to amend rules so a county board needs only a simple majority, not unanimity, to reject a voter's photo-ID exception affidavit for both in‑person and absentee‑by‑mail voting, changing how NC‑11 voters’ ballots can be rejected if they lack ID.[4]

NEWS.headline: GOP-led NC elections board votes to throw out ballots more easily
NEWS.relevanceSummary: This WRAL video segment describes new State Board rules that allow county boards to reject voter ID exception affidavits by majority vote and to more easily remove people deemed too noisy at polling places, altering both ballot acceptance standards and polling-place conduct rules statewide, including in NC‑11.[3]

NEWS.headline: 'We're supporting a lie': NC elections board votes to throw out ballots more easily
NEWS.relevanceSummary: WRAL reports that the State Board of Elections approved rules making it easier for county officials to reject voter ID exception affidavits by majority vote and to remove people from polling places for being disruptive, with additional rules planned to allow easier rejection of mail-in ballots over envelope technicalities.[6]

NEWS.headline: NC elections board approves new rule making it easier to disqualify ballots
NEWS.relevanceSummary: The State Board of Elections approved new voter ID rules so that county boards can now reject ballots from voters who use ID exception affidavits by a simple majority instead of unanimously, which directly affects how NC-11 voters’ ballots may be accepted or disqualified in future elections.[7]

LAST_REFRESH.summary: Automated refresh completed 2026-07-22 for NC-11. Sources checked: 7. LLM verifications: 5. 4 lastVerified bumps on unchanged sources.

WEEKLY_REPORT.summary: Initial build for NC-11, cloned from the WA-03 dashboard template ahead of the November 3, 2026 general election. Chuck Edwards (R, inc.) won the GOP primary with roughly 70%; Jamie Ager (D) won a 5-way Democratic primary with roughly 65% — neither triggered a second primary (30% threshold under SB 656). Unlike neighboring NC-01 and NC-03, NC-11's boundaries were unchanged by SB 249 / SL 2025-95 (Oct. 2025). Statewide-uniform general-election calendar: registration deadline Oct 9, early voting Oct 15-31 (with same-day registration only during this window), absentee request deadline Oct 20, Election Day Nov 3 (7:30 p.m. absentee receipt deadline, no postmark grace period per SB 747), ID-cure deadline noon Nov 12, county canvass Nov 13, state canvass Nov 24. North Carolina bans absentee drop boxes statewide (SB 747). A July 2, 2026 BPR analysis flagged NC-11 as a potential toss-up race, noted here as political framing rather than a procedural fact. All 19 sources verified as of July 9, 2026. All events and rules flagged needsReview pending county-specific (especially Buncombe) and legislative-text confirmation.
